locator: War Powers Resolution, Public Law 93-148, § 2(a), 7 November 1973; 87 Stat. 555; codified at 50 U.S.C. § 1541(a). Text confirmed against Cornell LII (https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/50/1541). Corrected 'joint resolution' to 'chapter' (codified form) and restored truncated final clause.
text: It is the purpose of this chapter to fulfill the intent of the framers of the Constitution of the United States and insure that the collective judgment of both the Congress and the President will apply to the introduction of United States Armed Forces into hostilities, or into situations where imminent involvement in hostilities is clearly indicated by the circumstances, and to the continued use of such forces in hostilities or in such situations.
reviewNote: Passage text directly supports claim united-states-after-vietnam-claim-001: WPR § 2(a)'s purpose clause ('collective judgment of both the Congress and the President will apply') institutionalizes the Vietnam-memory lesson as a legal requirement for shared war authority. Evidence role 'grounds' is correct. Text confirmed and corrected ('joint resolution' → 'chapter') against Cornell LII in evidence-review pass.

locator: Address to the Nation, President Jimmy Carter, 15 July 1979; archived at Miller Center, University of Virginia (https://millercenter.org/the-presidency/presidential-speeches/july-15-1979-crisis-confidence-speech). Text confirmed verbatim against Miller Center archive.
text: The erosion of our confidence in the future is threatening to destroy the social and the political fabric of America. The confidence that we have always had as a people is not simply some romantic dream or a proverb in a dusty book that we read just on the Fourth of July. It is the idea which founded our Nation and has guided our development as a people.
reviewNote: Passage text directly supports claim united-states-after-vietnam-claim-001: Carter's 'erosion of our confidence... threatening to destroy the social and political fabric' directly names the symbolic-order crisis the claim identifies, and frames it as a failure of the founding confidence rather than a temporary setback. Evidence role 'grounds' is correct. Text confirmed against Miller Center archive in evidence-review pass.

locator: War Powers Resolution, Public Law 93-148, § 5(b), 7 November 1973; 87 Stat. 555; codified at 50 U.S.C. § 1544(b). Text confirmed against Cornell LII (https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/50/1544).
text: Within sixty calendar days after a report is submitted or is required to be submitted pursuant to section 1543(a)(1) of this title, whichever is earlier, the President shall terminate any use of United States Armed Forces with respect to which such report was submitted (or required to be submitted), unless the Congress (1) has declared war or has enacted a specific authorization for such use of United States Armed Forces, (2) has extended by law such sixty-day period, or (3) is physically unable to meet as a result of an armed attack upon the United States.
reviewNote: Passage text directly supports claim united-states-after-vietnam-claim-002: WPR § 5(b)'s automatic 60-day withdrawal clock converts the purpose clause (passage-001) into a binding enforcement mechanism — the President must terminate forces unless Congress affirmatively acts. This is the constitutional-containment dimension of the Vietnam-memory institutionalization the claim describes. Evidence role 'grounds' is correct. Text confirmed against Cornell LII in evidence-review pass.

locator: Caspar W. Weinberger, Secretary of Defense, 'The Uses of Military Power,' address to the National Press Club, Washington, D.C., 28 November 1984; primary source at Office of the Historian, U.S. Department of State, Foreign Relations of the United States 1981–1988, vol. I, doc. 219 (https://history.state.gov/historicaldocuments/frus1981-88v01/d219); full text also archived at PBS FRONTLINE (https://www.pbs.org/wgbh/pages/frontline/shows/military/force/weinberger.html).
text: We cannot fight a battle with the Congress at home while asking our troops to win a war overseas or, as in the case of Vietnam, in effect asking our troops not to win, but just to be there. [...] Fifth, before the U.S. commits combat forces abroad, there must be some reasonable assurance we will have the support of the American people and their elected representatives in Congress. This support cannot be achieved unless we are candid in making clear the threats we face; the support cannot be sustained without continuing and close consultation.
reviewNote: Passage text directly supports claim united-states-after-vietnam-claim-002: Weinberger explicitly names Vietnam ('in effect asking our troops not to win, but just to be there') and codifies the lesson as a doctrine of pre-engagement public and congressional consent. This is the executive-doctrine layer of the two-level institutionalization the claim describes. Evidence role 'grounds' is correct. Text confirmed against State Dept FRUS 1981-88 vol. I doc. 219 and PBS FRONTLINE in evidence-review pass. Note: Weinberger Doctrine was doctrine, not law — documented as uncertainty factor.
